abstraction-with-base-interfaces.label: Abstraction with Base Interfaces
abstraction-with-base-interfaces.summary: Define the method names subclasses must provide so services can depend on behavior, not concrete classes.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Try it yourself
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: Complete `ReportItem.render()` as an abstract-style method and implement it in `MetricItem`.

ci-shipping-interface-practice.title: Use a delivery cost interface
ci-shipping-interface-practice.prompt: Build two shipping option classes that both provide `cost()`, then total them through a service.
ci-shipping-interface-practice.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

ci-shipping-interface-practice.starterCode:
from models.ground_shipping import GroundShipping
from models.express_shipping import ExpressShipping
from services.shipping import total_shipping

options = [GroundShipping(4), ExpressShipping(4)]
print(total_shipping(options))

sc-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-desi.title: Check the design choice
sc-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-desi.prompt: Which choice best matches the lesson goal for abstraction with base interfaces?
sc-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-desi.hint: Choose the option that keeps object responsibilities clear.

help.concept: Define the method names subclasses must provide so services can depend on behavior, not concrete classes.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer that reduces duplication without hiding behavior.
help.hint_2: A good OOP answer usually keeps behavior near the object that owns the data.

options.a: Put every class in main.py so imports are never needed.
options.b: Give each class or service a focused responsibility and import it where needed.
options.c: Avoid methods so every object exposes all of its data directly.
options.d: Copy the same logic into each subclass to avoid shared code.

try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.title: Create a base report interface
try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.prompt: Complete `ReportItem.render()` as an abstract-style method and implement it in `MetricItem`.
try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.starterCode:
from models.metric_item import MetricItem

item = MetricItem("Open tasks", 4)
print(item.render())

try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.title: Render objects through a shared interface
try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.prompt: Complete `render_report()` so it calls `.render()` on each item.
try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-abstraction-with-base-interfaces-ske.starterCode:
from models.metric_item import MetricItem
from services.reports import render_report

items = [MetricItem("Done", 3), MetricItem("Open", 2)]
print(render_report(items))

concept.title: Abstraction with Base Interfaces: the idea
concept.bodyMarkdown: **Abstraction with Base Interfaces** is not just syntax. In this course it is practiced inside small projects where one file owns one clear part of the program. Define the method names subclasses must provide so services can depend on behavior, not concrete classes.

multifile-shape.title: Abstraction with Base Interfaces: the file shape
multifile-shape.bodyMarkdown: The file layout matters. Models hold object state and behavior, services coordinate groups of objects, tests verify behavior, and `main.py` stays thin enough to read.

graded-habit.title: Abstraction with Base Interfaces: what the grader checks
graded-habit.bodyMarkdown: The grader checks behavior from the running project. A strong answer keeps imports working, keeps all files present, and proves the object behavior through output or assertions.
